heroOverview: Argininemia (ICD-10: E72.21), also called arginase 1 deficiency, is the rarest urea cycle disorder, affecting roughly 1 in 300,000 to 1,000,000 births worldwide. Unlike other urea cycle defects, argininemia typically does not present with acute hyperammonemic crises in the newborn period; instead, it causes a slowly progressive spastic diplegia, intellectual disability, and growth failure that emerges in early childhood as arginine and guanidino compounds accumulate. The underlying defect is a biallelic mutation in the ARG1 gene encoding arginase 1, the enzyme that converts arginine to urea and ornithine in the final step of the urea cycle. Without arginase 1, arginine accumulates to toxic levels and guanidino compounds derived from it damage the nervous system. Dietary arginine restriction combined with nitrogen scavengers keeps arginine levels near normal and halts neurological progression if begun before significant damage occurs.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 1 in 300,000–1,000,000 live births; one of the rarest urea cycle disorders (NORD, 2024)
keyStats.demographics: Autosomal recessive; equal sex distribution; symptoms typically emerge between ages 2 and 4 years, unlike other urea cycle disorders that present in the newborn period
keyStats.avgAge: Spastic diplegia and developmental regression usually noticed between ages 2–4; full diagnosis often delayed to age 3–7 years
keyStats.globalCases: Fewer than 1,000 patients formally diagnosed worldwide; true prevalence likely higher due to under-diagnosis

definition: Argininemia (ICD-10: E72.21) results from homozygous or compound heterozygous mutations in the ARG1 gene on chromosome 6q23, which encodes arginase 1, the hepatic and red blood cell enzyme responsible for the final step of the urea cycle: the hydrolysis of arginine to urea and ornithine. Loss of arginase 1 activity causes arginine to accumulate in plasma, cerebrospinal fluid, and tissues, with values typically 5–20 times the normal upper limit (normal < 150 μmol/L; untreated argininemia > 400–600 μmol/L). Excess arginine is diverted to the synthesis of guanidino compounds — guanidinoacetate, argininic acid, and homoarginine — several of which are neurotoxic and account for the progressive spastic diplegia and cognitive decline that characterise the condition. The urea cycle itself is partially retained because alternative pathways partially compensate, explaining why acute neonatal hyperammonemic crises are uncommon. The condition falls under metabolic medicine and endocrinology, with neurology as a critical co-managing specialty given the motor and cognitive complications. Argininemia is included in many expanded newborn screening panels through elevated arginine on tandem mass spectrometry of dried blood spot amino acids.

typesClassification.description: Most cases; biallelic ARG1 mutations eliminate arginase 1 activity entirely. Presents with progressive spastic diplegia starting before age 5, intellectual disability, seizures, and growth failure. Plasma arginine is markedly elevated.

Rare cases with residual arginase activity have attenuated biochemical and clinical phenotype; may present with milder cognitive impairment or be identified through newborn screening without frank neurological symptoms.

primarySymptoms: Progressive spastic diplegia — bilateral lower limb stiffness, scissor gait, and toe-walking — that worsens over years and is the hallmark neurological manifestation of argininemia.
Intellectual disability and developmental regression: children who met early milestones begin losing skills or fail to keep pace with peers, typically noted between ages 2 and 4.
Seizures, occurring in approximately 40–50% of affected children, ranging from focal motor seizures to generalised tonic-clonic episodes.
Growth failure and short stature, driven by the catabolic effects of chronic arginine accumulation and poor appetite.
Hyperactivity and behavioural difficulties, including attention deficit, irritability, and autistic features, which emerge in preschool years.
Spasticity extending to the upper limbs and trunk in older children and adults with advanced untreated or poorly controlled disease.
Microcephaly or failure of normal head growth in severely affected children as neurological damage progresses.
Dietary protein aversion — the child self-limits protein intake, intuitively reducing arginine load, a characteristic feature noted by parents before diagnosis.
Mild to moderate hyperammonemia during intercurrent illness, catabolic stress, or dietary protein loading; severe acute crises are less typical than in other urea cycle disorders.
Liver enlargement (hepatomegaly) in some patients, reflecting the metabolic burden on the liver.
earlyWarningSigns: A toddler who begins walking on tiptoes or with a stiff-legged gait without an obvious orthopaedic cause.
Regression in speech or motor skills in a child who had been progressing normally.
Spontaneous refusal to eat meat, eggs, or other protein-rich foods in a toddler or preschooler.
Elevated arginine on a newborn screening dried blood spot, prompting urgent confirmatory plasma amino acid measurement.
emergencySigns: Acute seizure cluster or status epilepticus in a child with known or suspected argininemia.
Sudden deterioration in consciousness or extreme lethargy during a febrile illness, suggesting acute hyperammonemia.
Plasma ammonia above 200 μmol/L (normal < 50 μmol/L in children) on an emergency blood test.
Rapid loss of already achieved motor skills over days rather than months, suggesting acute decompensation.

causes.description: Homozygous or compound heterozygous mutations in ARG1 on chromosome 6q23 eliminate arginase 1 activity. Over 60 pathogenic mutations have been described, including missense, nonsense, and splice-site variants. Even small amounts of residual arginase activity substantially mitigate disease severity.

causes.cause: Accumulation of arginine and guanidino compounds
causes.description: Without arginase 1, arginine accumulates to 5–20 times normal plasma levels and is metabolised by alternative pathways to guanidinoacetate, homoarginine, and argininic acid. These guanidino compounds inhibit neuronal chloride transporters, disrupt GABA signalling, and cause oxidative damage to corticospinal and cerebellar pathways, producing spastic diplegia.

Although acute hyperammonemia is less common in argininemia than in proximal urea cycle defects, the urea cycle is impaired at its final step, reducing urea production. During catabolic stress — fever, illness, surgery, prolonged fasting — nitrogen flux overwhelms the partial residual capacity and plasma ammonia rises acutely.

Arginine is normally cleaved to provide ornithine, a substrate for polyamine synthesis and a recycling substrate for the urea cycle. Arginase 1 deficiency depletes ornithine relative to arginine, potentially impairing cell proliferation and immune function.

riskFactors.description: Both copies of ARG1 must be mutant; carrier parents are clinically healthy. Each child of two carriers has a 25% risk of being affected.
Related parents have a higher probability of carrying the same ARG1 allele, increasing the risk of affected offspring.

Where arginine is not measured on the newborn dried blood spot panel, argininemia is typically diagnosed after neurological symptoms develop, by which time some irreversible spastic damage may already have occurred.

Unrestricted dietary protein provides excess arginine substrate, accelerating neurological damage. Natural protein restriction is the cornerstone of treatment; unguided high-protein feeding worsens outcome.

Fever and reduced oral intake rapidly raise arginine and ammonia levels. Emergency protocols — increasing glucose, stopping protein temporarily, and hospitalising if ammonia rises — are essential to prevent acute decompensation.
Argininemia is often confused with cerebral palsy or other causes of progressive spastic paraparesis; each year of diagnostic delay allows further guanidino-mediated neurotoxicity to accumulate.

affectedDemographics: Infants detected on expanded newborn screening before symptoms develop
Children aged 2–7 presenting with progressive spastic diplegia and developmental regression
Higher rates in populations with founder mutations or consanguinity
Adults with undiagnosed or undertreated argininemia presenting with progressive spastic paraparesis mimicking hereditary spastic paraplegia
diagnosisOverview: Argininemia is diagnosed biochemically by demonstrating elevated plasma arginine on amino acid analysis, typically more than 300–600 μmol/L (reference range below 150 μmol/L). In many countries, it is now detected at birth through expanded newborn screening measuring arginine on a tandem mass spectrometry dried blood spot panel. When clinical suspicion arises from progressive spastic diplegia or developmental regression, plasma amino acid analysis with specific measurement of arginine, ammonia, and guanidino compounds in urine and cerebrospinal fluid supports the diagnosis. Arginase enzyme activity can be measured in red blood cells or liver tissue; values typically fall below 1% of normal. ARG1 gene sequencing confirms the diagnosis and identifies mutations for family counselling. Brain MRI often shows corticospinal tract signal abnormalities, particularly in the posterior limb of the internal capsule and spinal cord, in patients with established neurological disease. The key differential diagnoses are cerebral palsy (no plasma arginine elevation), other urea cycle disorders (different amino acid patterns), and hereditary spastic paraplegia syndromes (normal amino acids and normal ammonia).

diagnosticTests.purpose: The primary biochemical test; marked elevation of plasma arginine above 300–600 μmol/L is diagnostic in the clinical context. Also shows secondary changes in other cycle intermediates.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A venous blood sample is sent to a specialist metabolic laboratory. Results take 3–7 days. Fasting is preferred to reduce post-prandial amino acid fluctuations.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for elevated arginine on the dried blood spot in the first days of life, enabling pre-symptomatic diagnosis before neurological damage accumulates.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A heel-prick sample taken at 24–72 hours; screened alongside other metabolic conditions. Positive results require confirmatory plasma amino acid measurement within days.

diagnosticTests.test: Red blood cell arginase enzyme assay
diagnosticTests.purpose: Directly measures arginase 1 enzyme activity in red blood cells; values below 1% of normal confirm the diagnosis and exclude partial deficiency or carrier status.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A tube of blood is processed and sent to a specialist enzyme laboratory. Results take 1–3 weeks. Red blood cells contain arginase 1 and can be used without a liver biopsy.

diagnosticTests.test: ARG1 gene sequencing and deletion analysis
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the pathogenic mutations for definitive confirmation, genotype-phenotype correlation, family carrier testing, and prenatal diagnosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood is sent for sequencing of the ARG1 gene; results take 2–6 weeks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Monitors for hyperammonemia during illness or metabolic decompensation; usually mildly elevated in stable argininemia but can rise significantly during catabolic stress.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood must be collected in an ice-cold tube and processed within 15–30 minutes to avoid falsely high values. Results are available within hours at most hospitals.

diagnosticTests.test: Brain MRI with diffusion and FLAIR sequences
diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses corticospinal tract damage, white matter signal changes, and cerebral atrophy; useful for staging neurological involvement and monitoring treatment response.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: MRI takes 30–60 minutes; younger children typically require sedation or general anaesthesia. No radiation is involved.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of argininemia targets two goals: reducing plasma arginine toward the normal range (ideally below 200 μmol/L) and preventing acute hyperammonemia during catabolism. The cornerstone is dietary natural protein restriction to limit arginine intake, supplemented by an arginine-free, essential amino acid formula to meet protein and nutrient needs without providing additional arginine substrate. Unlike other urea cycle disorders, phenylalanine-free and lysine-free supplements are not specifically indicated; the formula must be arginine-free. Nitrogen scavengers — sodium benzoate and sodium phenylbutyrate or its prodrug glycerol phenylbutyrate — provide alternative pathways for nitrogen excretion as hippurate and phenylacetylglutamine, respectively, reducing the nitrogen load that would otherwise drive arginine accumulation. Sodium phenylbutyrate also has an arginase-independent ammonia-lowering effect. These agents are particularly important during illness and acute decompensation. Emergency protocols during catabolic stress include stopping natural protein intake temporarily (for 24–48 hours only), infusing glucose and lipid to prevent catabolism, and administering intravenous nitrogen scavengers if arginine and ammonia are rising. Liver transplantation has been performed in some patients with refractory disease; the transplanted liver provides arginase 1 and corrects the primary enzymatic defect, normalising plasma arginine, though neurological damage already present may not reverse. Antiepileptic therapy manages seizures. Physiotherapy and orthotics address spasticity. Regular monitoring of plasma arginine (monthly in young children, quarterly in stable adults) and ammonia guides treatment adjustments.

medicalTreatments.name: Natural protein-restricted diet with arginine-free amino acid formula
medicalTreatments.description: Restricts dietary arginine by limiting natural protein from all sources (meat, dairy, eggs, legumes) while meeting nitrogen and essential amino acid needs via a specialist medical formula that is arginine-free and provides sufficient energy and micronutrients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In prospective series, dietary control lowering plasma arginine below 200 μmol/L significantly reduces guanidino compound accumulation; neurological stabilisation or modest improvement is documented in approximately 70% of treated patients, with greatest benefit when started pre-symptomatically.

medicalTreatments.name: Sodium phenylbutyrate (or glycerol phenylbutyrate)
medicalTreatments.description: A nitrogen scavenger that is converted to phenylacetate, which combines with glutamine to form phenylacetylglutamine excreted in the urine, reducing the ammonia and arginine burden. Given orally in divided doses throughout the day.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In controlled trials of urea cycle disorders, nitrogen scavengers reduce plasma ammonia by approximately 30–50% and decrease acute decompensation hospitalisations; specific argininemia data show plasma arginine reductions of 20–40% when added to dietary therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Sodium benzoate
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative nitrogen scavenger that conjugates with glycine to form hippurate, providing an additional route for nitrogen disposal. Used alongside or instead of phenylbutyrate, particularly in young children where palatability of phenylbutyrate is challenging.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces ammonia burden and complements dietary restriction; combined benzoate and phenylbutyrate therapy achieves plasma arginine below 200 μmol/L in roughly 60–70% of fully adherent patients in reported series.

medicalTreatments.name: Antiepileptic therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Seizure control using agents appropriate for the seizure type (most commonly valproate, levetiracetam, or lamotrigine). Valproate is used cautiously because it can worsen hyperammonemia in urea cycle disorders; specialist advice guides drug selection.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Seizure freedom or substantial reduction in frequency is achieved in approximately 60–70% of argininemia patients on appropriate antiepileptic monotherapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Emergency glucose and lipid infusion (inpatient)
medicalTreatments.description: During catabolic illness, intravenous 10% glucose (and lipid if tolerated) is given to halt endogenous protein catabolism, preventing the nitrogen surge that elevates arginine and ammonia. Natural protein is stopped for 24–48 hours maximum, then cautiously reintroduced.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In the broader urea cycle disorder literature, prompt initiation of emergency anabolic infusion during decompensation reduces ammonia peak levels by approximately 40–60% and significantly lowers rates of neurological sequelae from acute crises.

surgicalOptions.name: Liver transplantation
surgicalOptions.description: Performed in patients with refractory neurological progression or recurrent severe hyperammonemia despite maximal medical therapy. The transplanted liver provides functional arginase 1, correcting the primary biochemical defect. Plasma arginine normalises within days of transplant.
surgicalOptions.successRate: In case series, liver transplantation normalises plasma arginine in over 90% of recipients; neurological stabilisation is typical but existing spastic damage rarely reverses fully. Graft and patient survival mirror other metabolic liver transplants, with 5-year survival rates above 85%.

alternativeTreatments.name: Ribose and uridine supplementation
alternativeTreatments.description: Some metabolic physicians add uridine to the regimen to support pyrimidine synthesis, which is indirectly impaired in urea cycle disorders; evidence in argininemia specifically is limited to case reports.

alternativeTreatments.name: Physiotherapy and botulinum toxin for spasticity
alternativeTreatments.description: Regular physiotherapy reduces contractures and preserves motor function; botulinum toxin injections into spastic lower limb muscles can temporarily improve gait and are used alongside dietary management in children with established diplegia.

whySeeSpecialist: Argininemia requires care from a metabolic disease specialist because dietary prescription, formula selection, nitrogen scavenger dosing, and emergency protocols are highly individualised. Neurological assessment by a paediatric neurologist is needed to track disease progression and manage seizures safely. Orthopaedic and physiotherapy input addresses the spastic diplegia. General practitioners and community paediatricians typically lack the expertise to manage the biochemical and neurological complexity of this ultra-rare condition.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a paediatric metabolic centre with a dedicated urea cycle disorder programme. The team should include a metabolic physician, specialist metabolic dietitian experienced in low-arginine diets, paediatric neurologist, and physiotherapist with experience in spastic movement disorders. Ask whether the centre has an emergency protocol card that parents can give to any emergency department. Transition to an adult metabolic centre should be planned before the patient reaches 18.
preventionStrategies: Expanded newborn screening that includes tandem mass spectrometry measurement of arginine allows treatment to begin before neurological damage occurs — the single most impactful preventive measure.
Genetic carrier screening for couples with a family history; prenatal ARG1 gene testing or enzyme assay on chorionic villus samples is available.
Strict dietary adherence from diagnosis, keeping plasma arginine within target range, prevents progressive neurological deterioration.
Family education on emergency protocols to initiate anabolic therapy at home and seek hospital care early during any febrile illness.
lifestyleModifications: Follow the prescribed arginine-limited diet consistently; even brief dietary lapses raise plasma arginine and guanidino compound levels.
Take the prescribed arginine-free amino acid formula at all meals to ensure complete nutrition within the dietary restriction.
Avoid prolonged fasting; eat small, frequent meals to minimise catabolic nitrogen release.
Prepare and carry an emergency protocol letter for any hospital, clinic, or emergency department encounter.
Engage in daily physiotherapy exercises to maintain lower limb flexibility and strength, adapted for the degree of spasticity.
Wear ankle-foot orthoses (AFOs) as recommended by the orthotist to improve gait efficiency and reduce contracture risk.

recommended: Arginine-free essential amino acid formula to meet protein requirements without adding arginine
Low-arginine natural foods: fruits, most vegetables, low-protein pasta and bread made for metabolic diets
Adequate energy from carbohydrates and fats to minimise endogenous protein catabolism
Folate, vitamin B12, vitamin D, calcium, and iron supplementation to address micronutrient gaps from restricted natural protein
avoid: High-arginine foods: all meats, fish, poultry, dairy products, eggs, nuts, seeds, and legumes beyond the prescribed small allowance
Commercial high-protein supplements, protein bars, or dietary products not designed for metabolic disorders
Prolonged fasting or very low calorie intake, which triggers catabolism and arginine release from muscle

exerciseGuidelines: Physical activity is encouraged to the degree that spasticity permits. Hydrotherapy (pool-based physiotherapy) is particularly well tolerated and helps maintain muscle length, reduce tone, and improve cardiovascular fitness without high joint impact. Stretching programmes targeting hip adductors, hamstrings, and calf muscles should be done daily. Strenuous exercise that causes significant muscle breakdown should be approached cautiously, as it raises nitrogen flux and can transiently elevate arginine and ammonia; patients should be advised to stay well hydrated and eat carbohydrate-rich snacks before and after exercise sessions.
dailyManagement: Take nitrogen scavenger medications (sodium benzoate or phenylbutyrate) at every meal as prescribed.
Use the arginine-free amino acid formula at every feeding opportunity; do not skip doses.
Monitor for early signs of decompensation — vomiting, unusual lethargy, confusion, or worsening spasticity — and activate the emergency protocol immediately.
Attend monthly or quarterly plasma arginine and ammonia checks; bring the metabolic team up-to-date with any dietary changes at each visit.
Document daily formula and dietary intake for review by the dietitian; any weight loss or appetite drop should prompt early review.
Review anticonvulsant medication at least annually with the neurologist; adjust doses as the child grows.
prognosis: Prognosis in argininemia depends critically on when treatment starts and how well biochemical targets are maintained. Patients diagnosed through newborn screening and treated from the first weeks of life have the best outcomes: most reach school age with normal or near-normal motor function and cognitive development. Children diagnosed after spastic diplegia develops may stabilise with treatment but rarely fully regain lost function; existing corticospinal tract damage is largely irreversible. Untreated adults can develop a severe spastic quadriplegia requiring full-time care. With modern metabolic management, including dietary restriction, nitrogen scavengers, and emergency protocols, the majority of patients now survive into adulthood. Life expectancy with treated argininemia is substantially better than historical untreated cohorts, but long-term data are limited given the rarity of the condition.
recoveryTimeline: Plasma arginine falls toward target within days to weeks of initiating dietary restriction and nitrogen scavengers. Seizure frequency typically decreases within weeks to months of biochemical stabilisation. Existing spastic diplegia does not reverse but usually stabilises; some patients show modest motor improvement in the first 12 months after metabolic control is achieved. Developmental catch-up is possible in children diagnosed before significant neurological damage when treatment is comprehensive; newborn-screened patients typically maintain normal developmental trajectories.
complications: Progressive spastic diplegia and ultimately spastic quadriplegia if arginine remains uncontrolled into adulthood.
Intellectual disability and academic failure from chronic guanidino compound neurotoxicity in under-treated patients.
Epilepsy with medically refractory seizures in approximately 40–50% of affected individuals.
Growth failure and short stature from chronic catabolic stress and dietary restriction.
Acute hyperammonemia during catabolic illness, which can cause rapid neurological deterioration if not treated promptly.

supportResources.name: National Urea Cycle Disorders Foundation (NUCDF)
supportResources.description: US-based patient advocacy organisation providing education, family support, and research funding for argininemia and all urea cycle disorders.

supportResources.name: NORD — Argininemia Overview
supportResources.description: Plain-language overview of argininemia covering symptoms, diagnosis, and treatment from the National Organization for Rare Disorders.

supportResources.name: UCDC — Urea Cycle Disorders Consortium
supportResources.description: NIH-funded research network coordinating clinical studies and providing specialist referral guidance for urea cycle disorders including argininemia.

sources.title: Brusilow SW, Horwich AL. Urea Cycle Enzymes. In: Scriver CR et al., The Metabolic and Molecular Bases of Inherited Disease, 2001

confusedWithConditions.name: Other urea cycle disorders
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Other urea cycle disorders (OTC, CPS1, ASS1 deficiencies) typically cause severe neonatal hyperammonemic crises with low plasma arginine and citrulline, the opposite pattern to argininemia where arginine is markedly elevated and neonatal crises are uncommon.

confusedWithConditions.name: Citrullinemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Citrullinemia causes elevated plasma citrulline rather than arginine and presents as neonatal hyperammonemia with encephalopathy; arginine is low in citrullinemia, the opposite of argininemia.

confusedWithConditions.name: Phenylketonuria
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: PKU also causes intellectual disability detected on newborn screening, but it elevates plasma phenylalanine not arginine, causes no spastic diplegia, and is managed with phenylalanine restriction.

relatedConditions.name: Citrullinemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Another urea cycle disorder managed at the same specialist metabolic centres with overlapping nitrogen scavenger treatment strategies.

relatedConditions.name: Urea cycle disorders
relatedConditions.relevance: Argininemia is classified within the urea cycle disorder family; all involve defects in the same metabolic cycle and share some management principles.

relatedConditions.name: Phenylketonuria
relatedConditions.relevance: Like argininemia, PKU is an amino acid disorder detected on newborn screening and managed with lifelong dietary restriction and amino acid supplementation.

relatedConditions.name: Propionic acidemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Another organic acid disorder causing metabolic encephalopathy and neurological damage in infancy and childhood, managed with protein restriction and emergency protocols.

faqs.question: What is argininemia?
faqs.answer: Argininemia is a rare inherited disorder in which the enzyme arginase 1, needed for the final step of the urea cycle, is deficient or absent. The amino acid arginine and toxic compounds derived from it accumulate, causing progressive spastic diplegia, intellectual disability, and seizures from early childhood.

faqs.question: How is argininemia different from other urea cycle disorders?
faqs.answer: Most urea cycle disorders cause severe hyperammonemic crises in newborns. Argininemia is unusual: crises are uncommon in the newborn period, and the main problem is slow accumulation of arginine and guanidino compounds causing progressive neurological damage over years. It is also one of the rarest urea cycle disorders.

faqs.question: What causes the walking difficulty in argininemia?
faqs.answer: Excess arginine is converted to guanidino compounds that damage the corticospinal tracts — the nerve pathways from brain to spinal cord that control leg movement. This produces spastic diplegia: stiff, scissor-like leg movements. The damage typically begins before age 5 and worsens without treatment.

faqs.question: How is argininemia treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment combines a low-arginine diet (restricting protein-rich foods) with an arginine-free amino acid formula to provide essential nutrients. Nitrogen scavengers such as sodium benzoate or sodium phenylbutyrate provide alternative routes for nitrogen excretion. Together these reduce plasma arginine toward normal and halt neurological progression.

faqs.question: Is argininemia detected at birth?
faqs.answer: Many countries now include arginine measurement on expanded newborn blood spot screening panels using tandem mass spectrometry. A positive screen allows treatment to start before symptoms develop, which is the best way to prevent neurological damage.

faqs.question: Can the walking problems in argininemia be reversed?
faqs.answer: Spastic diplegia that has already developed rarely fully reverses even with treatment, though it may stabilise or improve modestly in the first year after biochemical control is achieved. Children diagnosed and treated before symptoms develop through newborn screening have a much better chance of normal motor development.

faqs.question: Does argininemia affect intelligence?
faqs.answer: In untreated or late-diagnosed patients, intellectual disability is common due to guanidino compound neurotoxicity. Children who start treatment through newborn screening before significant damage accumulates typically develop normal or near-normal intellectual function.

faqs.question: Does argininemia cause liver disease?
faqs.answer: Liver transplantation corrects the primary biochemical defect and is performed in severely affected patients. Before transplant, some patients have mild hepatomegaly (enlarged liver). Liver function tests are monitored as part of routine follow-up. Significant liver failure is uncommon in argininemia compared with some other metabolic liver diseases.

faqs.question: What happens during an acute crisis in argininemia?
faqs.answer: Acute decompensation, triggered by infection, surgery, or prolonged fasting, causes ammonia and arginine to rise rapidly. Symptoms include vomiting, lethargy, confusion, and seizures. Emergency treatment involves stopping natural protein intake, starting intravenous glucose to prevent catabolism, giving intravenous nitrogen scavengers, and monitoring ammonia closely.

faqs.question: Is liver transplantation a cure for argininemia?
faqs.answer: Liver transplantation provides arginase 1 and normalises plasma arginine within days. It is considered curative for the metabolic defect. However, neurological damage present before transplant typically does not reverse completely, so early treatment is always preferred to transplantation as a rescue strategy.

faqs.question: What foods should be avoided in argininemia?
faqs.answer: All high-arginine foods are restricted: meat, poultry, fish, eggs, dairy products, nuts, seeds, and legumes are limited to prescribed small amounts. The bulk of protein is provided by a specialist arginine-free amino acid formula. Fruits, vegetables, and low-protein foods are generally allowed.

faqs.question: How often is blood monitored in argininemia?
faqs.answer: Plasma arginine and ammonia levels are checked monthly in young children and quarterly in stable older patients. More frequent monitoring is needed during illness, after dose changes, or during pregnancy. Results guide adjustments to dietary prescription and nitrogen scavenger doses.

faqs.question: Can someone with argininemia live a normal life?
faqs.answer: With early detection through newborn screening and good long-term biochemical control, many patients with argininemia grow up with normal or near-normal cognitive and motor function and attend regular schools. Adults with well-treated argininemia can live independently, though lifelong medical follow-up and dietary treatment are required.

faqs.question: Does argininemia cause seizures?
faqs.answer: Seizures occur in roughly 40–50% of patients, caused by guanidino compound accumulation disrupting neuronal signalling. They are managed with antiepileptic medications chosen carefully to avoid drugs that worsen ammonia levels, such as high-dose valproate. Better metabolic control often reduces seizure frequency.

faqs.question: Is argininemia hereditary?
faqs.answer: Yes. Argininemia is autosomal recessive: a child must inherit one mutant ARG1 gene from each parent to be affected. Each pregnancy of two carrier parents has a 25% chance of producing an affected child. Carrier testing and prenatal diagnosis are available for families who request them.

faqs.question: How is argininemia diagnosed if newborn screening is not available?
faqs.answer: When newborn screening is unavailable, diagnosis is typically prompted by a child with progressive spastic diplegia, developmental regression, or unexplained seizures. Plasma amino acid analysis showing markedly elevated arginine (above 300–600 μmol/L) is the key diagnostic finding, confirmed by red blood cell arginase enzyme assay and ARG1 gene sequencing.

faqs.question: Why do children with argininemia sometimes refuse to eat protein?
faqs.answer: A self-protective reduction in protein intake is a recognised and striking feature of argininemia. Children often naturally avoid meat, dairy, and other high-protein foods before diagnosis, instinctively limiting arginine ingestion. This can be an important diagnostic clue when combined with developmental or motor concerns.

faqs.question: What is the role of physiotherapy in argininemia?
faqs.answer: Daily physiotherapy is essential to prevent joint contractures, maintain range of motion in spastic limbs, and preserve walking ability. Ankle-foot orthoses improve gait, hydrotherapy reduces spasticity, and botulinum toxin injections are used in some children. These interventions are adjuncts to metabolic treatment, not replacements for it.

faqs.question: Does exercise worsen argininemia?
faqs.answer: Vigorous exercise breaks down muscle protein, releasing amino acids including arginine, and can transiently elevate plasma arginine and ammonia. Moderate, sustained exercise is encouraged, but patients are advised to stay well hydrated, eat carbohydrate snacks before and after exercise, and report any post-exercise lethargy or vomiting to their metabolic team.

faqs.question: Are there clinical trials for argininemia?
faqs.answer: Research is ongoing into enzyme replacement therapy with pegylated arginase 1 (a form of arginase conjugated to polyethylene glycol), which can be given intravenously to supplement or replace the missing enzyme without liver transplantation. Gene therapy approaches are also in early preclinical development. Patients can search clinicaltrials.gov for currently recruiting studies.

metaDescription: Argininemia is a rare urea cycle disorder causing spastic diplegia and intellectual disability in children. Learn symptoms, diet treatment, and prognosis.
